expected_output.title: Draft — Owner update on DH-2 mobilization slip and proposed corrective action
expected_output.summary: Formal email draft to Owner Construction Lead disclosing the DH-2 14-day mobilization slip, proposing Option 1 corrective action (second steel-erection crew, ~$1.4M order-of-magnitude), and requesting feedback ahead of the May 8 internal decision meeting. Proposes only — no commitment.
expected_output.body_markdown:
Subject: DH-2 mobilization — 14-day slip and proposed corrective action

Dear Owner Construction Lead,

I am writing to update you on a schedule item on Building DH-2, ahead of our internal decision meeting on May 8.

DH-2 mobilization is currently forecast for 2026-06-01, fourteen days behind the baseline of 2026-05-18. As of the 2026-04-29 schedule pull, DH-2 critical-path total float stood at eight days, so the 14-day slip burns the float and pushes the critical path approximately six days. Long-lead equipment for DH-2 remains on or ahead of need-by — DH-2 switchgear was delivered on site 2026-04-30, and the DH-2 chiller plant is confirmed for 2026-10-22 against a 2026-11-10 need-by — so procurement is not the constraint. The IT-equipment handover commitment for DH-2 (2027-03-15) is not at risk in our current view, but the project-level buffer to that date narrows from approximately thirty days to approximately twenty-four days under the base case.

We have evaluated four corrective options. The proposed option, subject to your review and to the outcome of our internal decision meeting on May 8, is to crash Seq A with a second steel-erection crew. Based on DH-1 productivity benchmarks, this option is expected to recover approximately eight of the fourteen days, with the residual slip absorbed by float on interior MEP rough-in. The order-of-magnitude premium is approximately $1.4M (second crew, tower-crane second shift, and travel-trade uplift). We have confirmed second-crew availability with the New Albany trade base.

The overall program status for the week ending 2026-05-02 remains green; the formal weekly status update on May 9 will reflect both this slip and the corrective plan once internally approved.

## Decision needed

At this stage we are not asking the owner to approve the corrective option. We are asking for any constraints or preferences from your side that should inform our internal decision on May 8. If we hear nothing by 2026-05-07, we will proceed with the internal decision and follow up with the outcome on May 9.

--
Project Director
QPB1 Construction

expected_output.body_markdown:
Subject: DH-2 Seq A — possible second-crew engagement (heads-up; not an authorization)

Heads-up, not authorization to proceed.

DH-2 mobilization has slipped 14 days from the 2026-05-18 baseline to a forecast of 2026-06-01. We are evaluating, internally on 2026-05-08, an option to crash Seq A with a second steel-erection crew. Recovery target is approximately 8 of the 14 days, based on DH-1 productivity benchmarks.

If the decision approves the option, our preferred timeline is:

- Second-crew mobilization on site: 2026-05-25.
- Full ramp on Seq A: 2026-06-01.

To preserve the option, we need the following from you by 2026-05-09 (close of business):

1. Confirmation of second-crew availability for the May 25 mobilization, with the named foreman / general foreman.
2. Lead time you need from a written authorization to mobilization on site. We will need to lock our commitment with you by 2026-05-12 if the decision goes that way.
3. Preliminary cost band for the second-crew engagement (labor, per-diem, travel premium). Order-of-magnitude is fine; firm pricing follows on a written authorization.

No PO will be issued without a written authorization to proceed. Please direct your response to the Procurement Lead via this Procore thread.

--
Procurement Lead
QPB1 Construction

input.purpose: Counter-propose to Trane on the SUB-0488 chiller substitution package. Per the May 1 PM analysis, the $0 net cost-neutral claim is materially incomplete (~$1.4M of integration delta is not in the proposal) and the manufacturer rating shows IPLV 0.34 vs York basis-of-design 0.32 (6.25% efficiency loss; ~$3.6M lifetime NPV at 8%). Two conditions for further consideration: (1) Trane absorbs the integration delta; (2) Trane provides AHRI 550/590 certified test data demonstrating IPLV ≤ 0.33 kW/ton. Response requested in 14 days. Do not commit acceptance.
key_facts: SUB-0488 (2026-04-25) proposes 4× Trane CenTraVac CVHF-1500 in place of basis-of-design 4× York YK-1500 (Spec 23 64 23).
Vendor's $0 net-neutral claim excludes ~$240k freight delta, ~$1.1M mechanical integration (4 units × ~$275k header rework), and ~$80k controls integration — total ~$1.4M.
Manufacturer IPLV: York YK-1500 0.32 kW/ton (basis-of-design); Trane CVHF-1500 0.34 kW/ton (manufacturer rating).
Energy model 2025-08-12 used 0.32 kW/ton; the 0.02 kW/ton delta corresponds to ~$280k/yr energy cost; 25-yr NPV at 8% discount is ~$3.6M.
Trane delivery offered 2026-08-01 vs York confirmed 2026-10-22 against a 2026-11-10 need-by — schedule pull-in is not on the critical path.
York PO QPB1-CHL-002 ($48M) has a 50% milestone payment of approximately $22M scheduled for August 2026; restocking risk up to ~8% applies if York does not re-allocate.
EOR has not yet formally responded on substitution acceptability (RFI-00301 open).

expected_output.title: Draft — Trane counter-proposal on SUB-0488 chiller substitution
expected_output.summary: Direct email draft to the Trane National Account Manager. Documents the cost gap (~$1.4M integration not in vendor's $0 net-neutral claim) and the efficiency gap (IPLV 0.34 vs basis 0.32) and proposes two conditions for further consideration: Trane absorbs the integration delta and provides AHRI 550/590 certified test data demonstrating IPLV ≤ 0.33 kW/ton. Response requested in 14 days. No acceptance committed.
expected_output.body_markdown:
Subject: SUB-0488 — counter-proposal on the DH-2 chiller substitution package

Thank you for SUB-0488 dated 2026-04-25 proposing 4× Trane CenTraVac CVHF-1500 in place of the basis-of-design 4× York YK-1500 on QPB1 DH-2 (Spec 23 64 23).

We have completed our internal scope/cost/schedule/performance review. Two issues prevent us from accepting the package as written. We are not rejecting the substitution; we are asking Trane to address two specific conditions before we evaluate further.

## Cost gap

The $0 net cost-neutral claim does not appear to include the QPB1-side integration cost. Our preliminary build is:

- Freight delta (Wisconsin vs Pennsylvania OEM origin): approximately $240,000.
- Mechanical integration (different connection size on chilled-water headers per CVHF spec sheet): approximately $1.1M, based on four units × approximately $275,000 of field rework each.
- Controls integration (BACnet object map differences): approximately $80,000.

Total preliminary integration cost not currently in the proposal: approximately $1.4M.

## Performance gap

The energy model (dated 2025-08-12) was built on the York YK-1500 IPLV of 0.32 kW/ton. The published manufacturer rating for the CVHF-1500 is 0.34 kW/ton — a 6.25% efficiency loss at IPLV. At our DH-2 forecast load profile, the 0.02 kW/ton delta is approximately $280,000 per year of energy cost; over a 25-year horizon at an 8% discount rate, the net-present-value impact is approximately $3.6M.

## Schedule

The 12-week delivery improvement (Trane 2026-08-01 vs York 2026-10-22) does not change DH-2 critical path; the York delivery is already 19 days ahead of the 2026-11-10 need-by, and chiller install is gated by chiller-plant slab and overhead piping rather than chiller arrival.

## Two conditions for further consideration

1. **Integration cost.** Trane absorbs the approximately $1.4M of QPB1-side integration cost as part of the substitution package, with a firm itemized number replacing our preliminary build.

2. **Performance certification.** Trane provides AHRI 550/590 certified test data on the proposed CVHF-1500 evaporator configuration demonstrating an IPLV of 0.33 kW/ton or better.

If both conditions are met, we will return to internal evaluation. If either is not met, we expect to maintain the existing York order. Please send your response on both conditions by 2026-05-19.

To be explicit: this letter is not an acceptance of the substitution and is not a commitment to proceed. Acceptance, if any, will follow a written authorization on QPB1 letterhead.

--
Procurement Lead
QPB1 Construction

expected_output.body_markdown:
## Executive Summary

Class 5. The QPB1 concept screening package is 10 pages of program-and-massing material — cover sheet, site context map, five narrative pages, three massing diagrams. There is no structural, mechanical, electrical, plumbing, civil engineering, schedule, or specification content. The package supports order-of-magnitude screening (-50% / +100%) only.

## Class Recommendation

Class 5. Design maturity is roughly 1-2%, consistent with concept screening. To move to Class 4 the package needs a sheet set with site plan, gross floor plans, an MEP block diagram with capacity targets, and at least a programmatic equipment list.

## Supporting Evidence

- Scope completeness is low: cover sheet + 9 narrative/massing pages, no sheet list, no specs.
- Structural detail: none. No framing, no foundation concept beyond a footprint.
- Architectural detail: massing only, three options of which Option B is annotated as preferred.
- Mechanical / electrical / plumbing: not present.
- Site/civil: a single context map identifies acreage, zoning, and highway frontage.
- Specifications: none.
- Vendor data: none.
- Schedule: not present.

## Gaps and Missing Information

To unlock Class 4, the next deliverable should be a study-grade design set: site plan with grading, floor plans for both data halls, electrical one-line with gross MW capacity, mechanical block diagram with cooling capacity targets, and a programmatic equipment count. To unlock Class 3 from Class 4, add structural framing, full mechanical schedules, partial 3-part specs, and a vendor placeholder list.

## Files Inspected

QPB1-Concept-Screening.pdf — 10 pages, extraction OK. No sheet structure detected.

## Reviewer Notes

Nothing in the package warrants confidence above the band for Class 5. Reviewer should confirm the upload is complete; if more sheets exist on the user's desktop they were not included.

## Executive Summary

Class 4. The QPB1 DH-2 study set (62 sheets) supports feasibility-level pricing. It contains gross architectural floor plans, a graded site plan with cut/fill volumes, a mechanical block diagram with cooling capacity, and an electrical one-line at the gross MW level. It does not contain structural framing, equipment schedules, or specifications, which keeps it below Class 3.

## Class Recommendation

Class 4. Design maturity sits around 8-12%, near the middle of the 1-15% Class 4 band. Accuracy band -30% / +50%.

## Supporting Evidence

- Scope is broad: 62 sheets covering A, S (light), C, M, E. Sheet index is present.
- Architectural detail: gross floor plans for both data halls (L1 plus partial L2), no enlarged plans.
- Site/civil detail: grading plan with 1-ft contours; cut/fill 145,000 CY noted on sheet C-101.
- Mechanical detail: block diagram with 96 MW IT load, 4500 ton chiller plant N+2 (sheet M-001). No mechanical schedules.
- Electrical detail: one-line with dual 138 kV feeds, four 3000 kVA generators, 96 MW IT (sheet E-001). No feeder sizing.
- Structural detail: not present beyond foundation footprint outlines.
- Specifications: none.
- Schedules / vendor data: none.

## Gaps and Missing Information

To unlock Class 3, add structural framing for both data halls, mechanical schedules with chiller and CRAH capacities, electrical feeder sizing on the one-line, partial 3-part specs (Divisions 03/05/26/23 at minimum), and a vendor-placeholder equipment schedule. To unlock Class 2, layer in coordinated A/S/M/E/P, full specs, geotechnical report, and a commissioning plan.

## Files Inspected

QPB1-DH2-Study.pdf — 62 pages, extraction OK. Sheet index parsed cleanly; no addenda noted.

## Reviewer Notes

The set is well organized for a study-grade package and the engineering is internally consistent (96 MW IT load tied to both mechanical capacity and electrical service). Confidence is high that this is a Class 4 set — the main downward pressure is the absence of structural and specifications.

## Executive Summary

Class 3. The QPB1 DH-2 60% DD package is a 228-sheet PDF plus a coordinated A/S IFC model. The PDF carries mechanical schedules, electrical feeder sizing, and partial 3-part specs for Divisions 03/05/23/26. The IFC has 18,400 entities with quantity property sets (IfcQuantityArea, IfcQuantityVolume), enabling direct quantity takeoff on concrete (24,800 CY), structural steel, slab area, and partition counts. The set supports a control-budget estimate at -20% / +30%.

## Class Recommendation

Class 3. Design maturity ~30-35%, mid-band for Class 3. The IFC quality multiplier is meaningful here: structural and architectural quantities don't have to be inferred from sheets — they're directly readable. To get to Class 2, the package would need full specs across all divisions, vendor model numbers on the equipment schedules, geotechnical data, and a commissioning plan.

## Supporting Evidence

- Scope completeness: 228 sheets across A/S/M/E/P/C plus partial specs.
- Architectural detail: full L1/L2 plans, enlarged plans, RCPs.
- Structural detail: framing plans, column schedules, foundation plans.
- Mechanical detail: schedules for chillers, CRAHs, AHUs with capacities.
- Electrical detail: one-line with feeder sizing, panel schedules, MV layout.
- Plumbing/FP detail: present, less developed than M/E.
- Specifications: partial 3-part for Div 03 (concrete), 05 (steel), 23 (HVAC), 26 (electrical). Other divisions absent.
- IFC quantities: takeoff-feasible. Concrete 24,800 CY; slab area 1.84M SF.

## Gaps and Missing Information

To unlock Class 2: full 3-part specs across all CSI divisions; equipment vendor model numbers on M/E schedules; geotechnical report; commissioning plan; clash-free coordination report; vendor-issued shop drawing markups.

## Files Inspected

QPB1-DH2-60pct-DD.pdf — 228 pages, extraction OK. QPB1-DH2-Structural-A-S.ifc — 18,400 entities, OK, quantity property sets present.

## Reviewer Notes

The IFC raises confidence materially. Without it this would still be Class 3 but at the lower end; with it, structural quantity risk shrinks meaningfully. Reviewer should verify the IFC corresponds to the same revision as the PDF (cover sheet on PDF says 60% DD 2026-04-20; the IFC was extracted alongside).

## Executive Summary

Class 2. The QPB1 DH-2 90% CD package is a complete construction-document-grade set: 468 fully coordinated drawings, 612 pages of full 3-part specifications across CSI Divisions 02-33, a 142,000-entity IFC with vendor model numbers in the property sets, 118 pages of vendor cut sheets, and an 84-page geotechnical report. All major risk drivers for a control budget or bid baseline are addressable.

## Class Recommendation

Class 2. Design maturity ~65%, at the upper end of the Class 2 band. The package is approaching Class 1 territory, but per program rules we do not produce Class 1 classifications (full bid documentation is required). Reviewer should treat this as the upper bound of Class 2.

## Supporting Evidence

- Scope completeness: full coordinated A/S/M/E/P/FP/C/T plus addenda 1 and 2 noted on the cover sheet.
- Architectural detail: 468 coordinated sheets including enlarged plans, RCPs, partition types.
- Structural detail: framing, foundations, embeds; IFC carries 8.4M lb of structural steel quantity directly.
- Mechanical detail: chiller equipment (8 IfcChiller entities) with model numbers; full schedules.
- Electrical detail: full one-line with feeder sizes; switchgear layouts; vendor data for Schneider, Eaton.
- Plumbing/FP detail: present and coordinated.
- Specifications: full 3-part across CSI 02-33.
- Vendor data: 118 cut sheets with model numbers.
- Geotech: bearing 6,500 psf at -4 ft on SPT data; foundation contingency can be tightened.
- Quantity takeoff feasibility: very high; IFC quantities cover concrete (26,200 CY), steel (8.4M lb), area.

Gaps to a Class 1 estimate (out of scope but listed for completeness): final bid set with addenda integrated, bid clarifications log, finalized commissioning plan, signed/sealed final structural. None of these gaps are listed in `missing_for_next_class` because Class 1 is not generated.

## Files Inspected

Five files; all extraction OK. Drawings + specs + coordinated IFC + vendor data + geotech.

## Reviewer Notes

This is the upper-bound Class 2 case. The set is essentially bid-ready on engineering content. Estimator-scheduler can now anchor on hard quantities for concrete, steel, slab area, and vendor-line equipment.

expected_output.title: Class 5 estimate + Level 1 schedule — QPB1 Site Selection Concept
expected_output.summary: Class 5 ROM. 96 MW IT-load hyperscale DC at $1.10B base + indirects + 30% contingency = $1.50B. Level 1 schedule, ~36 months to substantial completion. Single biggest risk: scope undefined.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Executive Summary

For internal use unless approved for external distribution. Class 5 ROM for QPB1 at the site-screening stage. Total $1.50B at 96 MW IT load, ~$15.6M/MW, accuracy band -50% / +100%. Schedule is Level 1 — ~36 months from NTP to substantial completion. The estimate is dominated by an LLM-generated $/MW benchmark; reviewers should treat numbers as order-of-magnitude only.

## Estimate

One ROM line based on $/MW benchmark at 96 MW. Direct $1.10B; indirects $90M (8%); contingency $358M (30%); escalation $-50M (negative because base year sits later than midpoint adjustment in this draft, see Basis of Estimate). Total $1.50B.

## Schedule

Level 1, six summary activities: 1) Site selection complete; 2) Permit + design through DD; 3) Procure long-lead equipment; 4) Site civil; 5) Building shell + MEP; 6) Commissioning + substantial completion. Total ~1,090 calendar days (~36 months). Critical path runs through long-lead chillers and MV gear procurement.

## Basis of Estimate

Single-row ROM at $11.5M/MW × 96 MW = $1.10B direct, sourced from cost library v0.1.0 row 01 00 00. Library row is `llm_estimate` with confidence 0.45 — this is the appropriate provenance for a Class 5 ROM with no design detail. Indirects at 8% reflect typical hyperscale-DC owner indirects (permitting, owner CM, FF&E, soft costs). Contingency at 30% sits at the upper end of the Class 5 band per AACE 18R-97 because the design is at 1-2% completion. Escalation is computed at 4%/year from a 2026 base year to a 2027 midpoint; net is small relative to the ROM scale.

## Basis of Schedule

Level 1 summary, six activities. Durations are programmatic: design and permit through DD ≈ 12 months, long-lead procurement ≈ 18 months running concurrent with site civil ≈ 12 months, building shell + MEP ≈ 18 months, Cx + SC ≈ 6 months. The chiller and MV gear procurement chain controls the critical path because design hasn't progressed enough to commit alternative concurrency strategies.

## Risk Register

6 program-level risks: scope undefined (highest), permitting timeline, MV utility coordination, long-lead chiller delivery, site geotech unknown, regional labor.

## Contingency

30% of direct + indirect = $358M. Sits at the upper end of the Class 5 band (-50%/+100%) and reflects scope undefinition — the package is 1-2% design.

## Missing Information to Next Class

Unlocking Class 4 needs a study-grade site plan with grading, gross floor plans, MEP block diagrams with capacity targets, and a programmatic equipment list. Estimated cost to deliver: $400-700K of design fee.

expected_output.summary: Class 4 study estimate for QPB1 DH-2 at 96 MW IT load. Direct $0.96B + indirects + 20% contingency = $1.36B. Level 2 phase schedule, ~38 months. Top risk: structural undefined.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Executive Summary

For internal use unless approved for external distribution. Class 4 study estimate for QPB1 DH-2 at 96 MW IT load. Direct cost $0.96B; indirects 9% = $86.4M; contingency 20% = $209M; escalation 4%/yr to 2027 midpoint = $40M. Total $1.36B (~$14.2M/MW). Level 2 phase schedule with 32 activities, ~1,160 calendar days. Critical path runs through MV procurement and chiller delivery.

## Estimate

Four primary cost-code rows derived from study-set capacity figures: earthwork (145,000 CY at $12/CY = $1.74M), chillers (4500 tons at $1,450/TON = $6.53M), generators (4 EA at $1.9M = $7.6M), transformers (4 EA at $1.2M = $4.8M). Balance of plant rolled up via $/MW benchmark for everything else not yet itemizable.

## Schedule

Level 2, 32 activities covering Permitting, Design through DD/CD, Procurement (long-lead), Site civil, Foundations, Structure, MEP rough-in, MEP finishes, Commissioning, Substantial completion. Total 1,160 calendar days. Critical path through MV gear and chiller procurement.

## Basis of Estimate

Study-set takeoffs: 145,000 CY cut/fill (sheet C-101); 4,500 TON cooling, N+2 (sheet M-001); 4 generators 3000 kVA (sheet E-001); 4 pad-mount transformers (138-13.8 kV) implied by dual-feed one-line. Balance of plant captured via remaining-MW benchmark at $11.5M/MW × ~80 MW (96 MW IT minus the items priced explicitly above contributing ~16 MW worth of unpriced scope offset). All rates from cost library v0.1.0; all are llm_estimate at 0.45-0.55 confidence. Escalation 4%/yr base 2026 to 2027 midpoint. Contingency 20% reflects Class 4 (-30%/+50%) accuracy band.

## Basis of Schedule

Level 2 phase-CPM with 32 activities. NTP target 2026-09-01. Permitting + design through DD ≈ 12 months. Procurement of long-lead items (chillers, MV gear) starts at DD-90 to enable concurrent civil. Site civil + foundations 9 months. Structure + MEP rough-in 12 months. MEP finishes 6 months. Cx + SC 4 months. Calendar 5x10 with Central Ohio weather (28 lost days/yr).

## Risk Register

12 phase-level risks; structural undefined is the single biggest cost-variance driver because no framing has been produced.

## Contingency

20% of direct + indirect = $209M. Class 4 band typical. Rationale: scope-completeness is 45% (study-grade) but no structural detail; large fraction of the estimate is llm_estimate.

## Missing Information to Next Class

Unlock Class 3 with structural framing, mechanical schedules, electrical feeder sizing, and partial 3-part specs. Approximate design fee to deliver: $2-3M.

input.context: QPB1 — New Albany hyperscale data center, Phase 1, DH-2 building. On 2026-07-09 the original mechanical vendor (Trane) notified the procurement lead of a 9-week slip on the four water-cooled centrifugal chillers (CWC-DH2-1 through -4) due to a compressor casting shortage at their Pueblo plant. Need-by for set-in-place was 2026-08-30 to support DH-2 commissioning level-3 IST starting 2026-10-01. Slip would push commissioning to 2026-12-08, blocking IT-load energization by ~6 weeks. The mechanical lead and the commissioning agent reviewed alternates 2026-07-10 through 2026-07-13. Two paths considered: (a) air-cooled VRF substitution (Daikin VRV-X), in stock with 4-week lead, ~$2.1M premium versus baseline plus $400k for revised piping/electrical, ~3% lower part-load efficiency; (b) hold to baseline and accept the 6-week energization slip with $1.8M/week of contingency burn at the projected occupancy. Owner PM directive 2026-07-14 selected (a). Approved at $2.5M total premium. Submittal SUB-0588 covers the substitution package; PCO-0042 covers the cost change; RFI-00342 closed the underslab piping change. Approval signed by owner PM 2026-07-14 17:30 ET.

expected_output.title: Chiller substitution — DH-2 air-cooled VRF in lieu of water-cooled centrifugal to absorb 9-week vendor slip
expected_output.summary: DH-2 mechanical substituted four water-cooled centrifugal chillers (Trane) with air-cooled VRF (Daikin VRV-X) on 2026-07-14 to absorb a 9-week vendor casting slip and protect IT-load energization. Cost premium $2.5M; ~3% part-load efficiency penalty.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Decision

On 2026-07-14 the owner PM authorized substituting the four water-cooled centrifugal chillers (CWC-DH2-1 through -4, originally Trane) with air-cooled VRF equipment (Daikin VRV-X) for DH-2 building mechanical. The substitution absorbs a 9-week vendor slip on compressor castings and preserves the IT-load energization date.

## Context

QPB1 DH-2 building required four 800-ton water-cooled centrifugal chillers set in place by 2026-08-30 to support commissioning level-3 IST starting 2026-10-01. On 2026-07-09 the original vendor reported a 9-week slip driven by a compressor casting shortage at their Pueblo plant (DFR-2026-07-13). Holding to baseline would have pushed commissioning to 2026-12-08 and the IT-load energization date by ~6 weeks. Mechanical lead and commissioning agent evaluated alternates 2026-07-10 to 2026-07-13.

Two paths were considered: (a) air-cooled VRF substitution, in stock with a 4-week lead and a $2.5M total premium ($2.1M equipment + $400k revised piping/electrical) at ~3% lower part-load efficiency; (b) hold to baseline at a projected $1.8M/week contingency burn during the 6-week energization slip. Path (a) was selected.

## Reasoning

- Schedule preservation was the deciding factor. Energization-date slip carried direct contingency burn plus owner-side IT-deployment exposure.
- Cost premium ($2.5M) was below the projected contingency burn of ~$10.8M over a 6-week slip.
- Part-load efficiency penalty (~3%) was acceptable at the operating profile DH-2 is sized for; the commissioning agent confirmed no design-load violation.
- Air-cooled VRF removes the cooling tower / condenser-water loop dependency, simplifying commissioning sequencing — a smaller, recoverable benefit.
- Owner PM held final authority; the directive is documented in PCO-0042.

## Follow-ups

- Submittal SUB-0588 covers the full substitution package (equipment, piping, electrical revisions). Status: in review at log time.
- RFI-00342 closed the underslab piping change for the air-cooled feed.
- Risk register entry RISK-R-014 (chiller delivery slip) is closed; opening RISK-R-022 to track VRF startup-tuning risk.
- Lifecycle-cost analysis of the part-load efficiency delta to be completed before commissioning IST level-4.

## References

- PCO-0042 — owner-directed substitution + cost premium $2.5M, approved 2026-07-14.
- SUB-0588 — air-cooled VRF substitution package.
- RFI-00342 — underslab piping change for VRF feed (closed).
- DFR-2026-07-13 — original vendor slip notice.
- RISK-R-014 — chiller delivery slip, closed by this decision.

metadata.decision_or_lesson: When a long-lead vendor slip threatens commissioning, an in-stock substitution at a defined cost premium can be the right call if the contingency burn from holding to baseline exceeds the substitution premium. Document the cost / efficiency / schedule trade with the owner directive on the same day to keep the trail clean.

Owner PM authorized substituting to air-cooled VRF (Daikin VRV-X) on 2026-07-14 at a $2.5M premium with ~3% part-load efficiency penalty.

input.context: QPB1 — Bldg-2 (DH-2) MEP rough-in. Near-miss reported 2026-08-21 14:42 ET. Two electricians from EAS-Electric (subcontractor foreman: Mike Donovan, cell 614-555-0102, email mdonovan@eas-electric.com) were preparing to install bus-duct sections on switchgear feeder SWB-DH2-1A. Per project LOTO procedure SOP-LOTO-005, dual lockout was required (utility-side disconnect + downstream feeder breaker). Field crew applied locks to the downstream breaker only. The utility-side disconnect was missed because the breaker schedule sheet had been re-printed without the upstream lockout point highlighted. A second-line walk by the GC superintendent at 14:42 caught the missing upstream lock before the crew opened the bus-duct enclosure. No contact with energized parts. No injury. OSHA log entry: NEAR-MISS-NM-0117. Stand-down conducted same day 15:30-16:30 with all electricians. Root cause investigation completed 2026-08-25, owned by safety director. Findings: (1) breaker schedule sheet was re-printed 2026-08-19 from a stale source missing upstream lockout-point markers, (2) crew did not cross-check against SOP-LOTO-005 before energy isolation, (3) JHA card for the activity referenced an older revision of the SOP. Corrective actions logged in CA-0044: schedule sheets are now re-issued only via document-control workflow; JHA cards must reference SOP revision number; weekly LOTO audit added to the safety director's recurring tasks.
input.decision_or_lesson: When a controlled-document re-print drops critical markup (e.g. a lockout point on a breaker schedule), the crew's procedural cross-check is the only remaining barrier. Cross-checks must be enforced via JHA-card SOP-revision binding, not crew memory. Three corrective actions logged in CA-0044.

expected_output.summary: On 2026-08-21 a Bldg-2 bus-duct LOTO was caught missing the utility-side lock before energy isolation. Root cause: a re-printed breaker schedule dropped the upstream lockout marker; crew did not cross-check against SOP. No injury. Three corrective actions logged in CA-0044.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Incident

On 2026-08-21 at 14:42 ET, a near-miss occurred during bus-duct installation prep on switchgear feeder SWB-DH2-1A in DH-2. The crew applied locks to the downstream breaker only; the utility-side disconnect was missed. A second-line walk by the GC superintendent caught the missing upstream lock before the bus-duct enclosure was opened. No contact with energized parts. No injury. OSHA log entry NEAR-MISS-NM-0117.

## Context

QPB1 DH-2 MEP rough-in. The subcontractor crew was working under JHA-EAS-0233 against project LOTO procedure SOP-LOTO-005 (dual lockout: utility-side disconnect plus downstream feeder breaker). The breaker schedule sheet referenced by the crew had been re-printed on 2026-08-19 from a stale source that no longer highlighted the upstream lockout point. The JHA card referenced an older revision of SOP-LOTO-005 that did not require explicit cross-check of upstream isolation.

## Root cause

- A controlled-document re-print bypassed the document-control workflow and produced a sheet missing the upstream-lockout marker.
- The JHA card was bound to a stale SOP revision; the crew was not prompted to cross-check the live procedure.
- Crew did not independently verify isolation against SOP-LOTO-005 before energy-isolation steps. Procedural cross-check was the only remaining defense after the document-control gap, and it was not exercised.

## Follow-ups

Three corrective actions logged in CA-0044, owned by the safety director:

- Re-print of any controlled lockout / isolation document is now permitted only via the project document-control workflow; ad-hoc re-prints are prohibited.
- All JHA cards must reference the SOP revision number explicitly; cards bound to stale revisions are rejected at the daily plan-of-the-day review.
- Weekly LOTO audit added to the safety director's recurring tasks; sample target: ≥3 active LOTOs reviewed per week against the live SOP.

A full-crew LOTO stand-down was conducted same day 2026-08-21 from 15:30 to 16:30 ET. Root-cause investigation closed 2026-08-25.

## References

- NEAR-MISS-NM-0117 — OSHA log entry.
- SOP-LOTO-005 — project lockout/tagout procedure (dual-isolation).
- JHA-EAS-0233 — job hazard analysis card for the activity.
- CA-0044 — corrective-action register entry.
- DFR-2026-08-21 — daily field report capturing the near-miss and stand-down.

metadata.decision_or_lesson: When a controlled-document re-print drops critical markup (e.g. a lockout point on a breaker schedule), the crew's procedural cross-check is the only remaining barrier to a live-energy contact event. Cross-checks must be enforced via explicit JHA-card-to-SOP-revision binding plus a recurring third-party audit, not crew memory.

GC superintendent caught the gap before the bus-duct enclosure was opened. No contact with energized parts; no injury. OSHA log NEAR-MISS-NM-0117. Three corrective actions logged in CA-0044.

expected_output.body_markdown:
## Milestone

DH-1 (Bldg-1) foundations completed on 2026-04-20, four days ahead of baseline 2026-04-24. Total volume: 14,200 cy across 22 mat pours and 86 column footings. Zero recordable safety incidents in the foundations phase. Approval to advance to the vertical phase was signed by the GC superintendent on 2026-04-22.

## Context

Foundations phase ran from 2026-02-09 to 2026-04-20 across mat foundations, column footings, and underslab MEP coordination on DH-1. The phase included two engineering refinements (RFI-00198 mat-edge bar termination, RFI-00203 anchor-bolt spacing under feeder taps) and one cost change (PCO-0033 added two cold-pour insulating blankets after a forecast-driven late-March cold spell).

## What worked

- **Two pump stations instead of one with extended reach.** Cut peak placement times ~25% and reduced cold-joint exposure on hot days. The added $185k rental cost was offset by ~$310k of overtime savings per the cost lead's analysis on DFR-2026-04-19. Net ~$125k positive.
- **BIM-integrated rebar scan with Hilti PS-1000.** Caught two field-fit conflicts at columns C-7 and C-12 before pour, avoiding two chip-and-redo events at ~$80k each. The integration also produced an as-built rebar layer that fed downstream MEP coordination cleanly.
- **Zero recordables.** Foundations phase ran 71 days at zero OSHA-recordable incidents, supported by daily JHA cards bound to live SOP revisions and a weekly safety walk by the safety director.

## What didn't

- **Underslab MEP coordination cadence slipped to bi-weekly in April.** A conduit-to-rebar conflict at column C-7 was first flagged in a weekly meeting and only escalated 9 days later in the next bi-weekly. The lesson: underslab MEP coordination stays weekly through MEP rough-in. Bi-weekly is acceptable only after MEP rough-in is closed and field-fit risk is low.
- **PCO-0033 cold-pour blankets** were a reactive add. Forecast-driven blanket reservation should be standing in the foundations phase plan for any pour window with overnight low < 25°F, not a PCO event.

## Follow-ups

- Underslab MEP coordination cadence — locked at weekly through DH-2, DH-3 MEP rough-in. Standing item on the schedule lead's recurring tasks.
- Cold-pour blanket reservation — added as a default phase-plan line item for foundations pours when forecast overnight low < 25°F (no PCO required).
- Two-pump strategy and Hilti PS-1000 rebar-scan workflow — propagate to DH-2 and DH-3 foundations playbooks.

## References

- MILESTONE-FND-DH1 — milestone closeout artifact, signed 2026-04-22.
- DFR-2026-04-19 — cost lead's pump-station economics analysis.
- RFI-00198 — mat-edge bar termination, closed in phase.
- RFI-00203 — anchor-bolt spacing under feeder taps, closed in phase.
- PCO-0033 — cold-pour blanket reactive add.

## Weekly review

Week of 2026-09-07 through 2026-09-13. Critical-path review meeting on 2026-09-12 14:00 ET, owned by the schedule lead. Three patterns of cross-cutting interest captured here for follow-up entries and runtime rule updates.

## Context

QPB1 was in active commissioning on DH-1 and continued MEP rough-in / vertical work on DH-2 during this period. Three artifacts anchor this entry: a milestone (DH-1 IST level-4), a vendor FAT (T-DH2-1), and an inter-agent reconciliation (procurement vs. DFR) closed by RFI-00410.

## What worked

- **Early CX engagement during MEP rough-in is repeatable.** DH-1 commissioning IST level-4 passed 2026-09-10, five days ahead of baseline (DFR-2026-09-10, MILESTONE-IST-DH1-L4). The pattern: the commissioning agent walks each MEP rough-in with the trade foreman as the install happens, rather than after. Punchlist items are caught while the trade is still on station, not after demobilization. The early-CX schedule benefit is now visible on DH-1; propagate the workflow to DH-2 and DH-3.
- **Conditional FAT acceptance with site rework verification has now worked twice.** T-DH1-1 transformer FAT in May 2026 (FAT-T-DH1-1) and T-DH2-1 this week (FAT-T-DH2-1) both closed by accepting the unit conditionally for ship and verifying punchlist rework at site receipt. Both transformers were instrumentation / calibration items that did not affect dielectric or insulation acceptance. The pattern preserved schedule without compromising the safety-critical FAT criteria. Procurement lead is documenting the criteria under which this is acceptable (no impact to dielectric, insulation, primary protection, or core acceptance) for use as a standing playbook entry.

## What didn't

- **Procurement-watch 'booked' status was overly permissive.** The daily-brief agent surfaced a conflict between procurement (chiller air-freight booked) and the DFR (not confirmed). The conflict was real: the vendor had marked the line booked when only the carrier reservation was held; the freight-forwarder pickup confirmation had not landed. Reconciliation rule updated via RFI-00410: 'booked' now requires both carrier reservation AND freight-forwarder pickup confirmation. Procurement-watch agent rule update is a runtime change, not a knowledge-entry change.

## Follow-ups

- Propagate early-CX-during-rough-in workflow to DH-2 and DH-3 commissioning playbooks. Owner: commissioning agent.
- Document the conditional-FAT-acceptance criteria (no impact to dielectric / insulation / primary protection / core acceptance) as a standing playbook entry. Open a follow-up knowledge entry once the criteria are written. Owner: procurement lead.
- Procurement-watch 'booked' status rule update applied via RFI-00410. Verify the runtime reconciliation behavior on next inter-agent conflict. Owner: schedule lead.
- Multi-event weekly review — three distinct patterns captured here. Each may warrant its own knowledge entry once the playbook updates are written.

## References

- DFR-2026-09-10 — DH-1 IST L4 pass-day field report.
- MILESTONE-IST-DH1-L4 — IST level-4 milestone closeout.
- FAT-T-DH2-1 — current week conditional acceptance.
- FAT-T-DH1-1 — May 2026 conditional acceptance, used for pattern comparison.
- RFI-00410 — procurement-vs-DFR reconciliation rule update.

expected_output.summary: Step-by-step SOP for pre-inspection of structural concrete pours ≥150 cy at the QPB1 Generator Yard slabs. Defines roles, acceptance criteria, and stop-work conditions per spec 03 30 00 §3.5.
expected_output.body_markdown:
# SOP — Concrete Pour Pre-Inspection (QPB1 Generator Yard Slabs)

## Scope
QPB1 Generator Yard structural slabs (DH-1 and DH-2). Applies to all structural concrete pours ≥150 cy. Aligns with spec 03 30 00 §3.5 and reinforcement plan S-201, Rev. 4.

## Owner
Site Superintendent (accountable). Concrete Foreman executes the pre-pour walk; Owner Inspector witnesses.

## Review cadence
Reviewed after each Generator Yard pour and at the end of QPB1 substructure phase.

## Steps

1. **Confirm pour authorization.** Concrete Foreman verifies that the pour is on the approved 7-day look-ahead and that the mix design (per approved submittal SUB-0411) matches the specified 5000 psi normal-weight mix. *Acceptance:* signed pour ticket reflects SUB-0411 mix; pour appears on look-ahead.
2. **Inspect formwork.** Concrete Foreman walks the entire form perimeter, checks for plumb (±1/4" over 8'), tightness of ties, bracing, and clean release agent. *Acceptance:* form deflection within tolerance; no loose ties; no debris in the form.
3. **Verify reinforcement.** QC Engineer checks bar size (#6), spacing (12" o.c. each way, top and bottom), cover, and chair support per S-201 Rev. 4. *Acceptance:* spot-check of 5 randomly selected grid intersections passes; cover verified on all four edges.
4. **Verify embeds and sleeves.** QC Engineer cross-checks embed and sleeve placement against E-401 and M-301 embed schedules. *Acceptance:* every scheduled embed and sleeve is in place and labeled; no unauthorized penetrations.
5. **Weather check.** Site Superintendent reviews the 24-hour forecast at pour start time. Stop-work if forecast exceeds 90°F ambient, predicts ≥0.25" rain in the placement window, or wind ≥25 mph for tower-pump operations. *Acceptance:* signed weather check on the pre-pour checklist.
6. **Pre-pour QC sign-off.** Site Superintendent signs the pre-pour QC checklist; Owner Inspector witnesses and counter-signs for pours ≥150 cy per spec §3.5. *Acceptance:* both signatures present and timestamped before the first truck arrives.
7. **Stop-work conditions.** Any of the following triggers stop-work: missing Owner Inspector witness, formwork or rebar non-conformance not closed in writing, mix-design slump or air at the truck out of tolerance on first three trucks, or weather check fails. Stop-work is called by the Site Superintendent or the Owner Inspector and logged in the DFR.

## References
- Spec 03 30 00 §3.5 Pre-Pour Requirements
- Drawing S-201, Rev. 4 (2026-04-12) — Generator Yard Slab Reinforcement Plan
- SUB-0411 — Approved mix design (5000 psi normal-weight)
- DFR 2026-04-28 — Generator Yard pour 1 of 4 (precedent walk completed)

# RACI — RFI Lifecycle Management (QPB1)

## Scope
QPB1 program-wide RFI workflow from issuance through closeout, all disciplines. Aligned with PMO-PROC-014 and the action item from the PMO Weekly on 2026-05-01.

## Roles
- Field Engineer (FE)
- Discipline Lead (DL)
- Site Superintendent (SS)
- PMO Coordinator (PMC)
- A/E of Record (AE)
- QC Engineer (QC)
- BIM Coordinator (BIM)

## Activities
1. Draft the RFI
2. Technical review of draft
3. Sponsor & approve issuance
4. Submit to A/E in Procore
5. Track RFI aging and chase A/E
6. Receive and disseminate A/E response
7. Close out RFI in Procore
8. Update model and spec record

## Matrix

| Activity | FE | DL | SS | PMC | AE | QC | BIM |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Draft the RFI | R, A | C | I | I |  | I |  |
| Technical review of draft | C | R, A | I | I |  | C |  |
| Sponsor & approve issuance | I | C | R, A | C |  | I |  |
| Submit to A/E in Procore | I | I | C | R, A | I |  |  |
| Track RFI aging and chase A/E | I | C | C | R, A | I |  |  |
| Receive and disseminate A/E response | I | C | I | R, A | I | I | I |
| Close out RFI in Procore | I | C | C | R | I | A |  |
| Update model and spec record | I | C | I | I |  | C | R, A |

## Notes
- Boundary previously confused (PMO Weekly 2026-05-01): **Site Superintendent sponsors and approves issuance; PMO Coordinator owns submittal to A/E and aging tracking.** This is the binding answer.
- QC Engineer is Accountable for closeout because closeout requires verification that the field implementation matches the A/E response.
- BIM Coordinator is Accountable for model/spec updates so that the as-designed record stays in sync with answered RFIs.
- All activities have one Accountable role and at least one Responsible role; no missing accountability.

input.question: What is the schedule risk if Building 2 (DH-2) mobilization slips two weeks from baseline (May 18) to June 1, 2026? Specifically, what does it do to the critical path, the IT-handover commitment in March 2027, and what recovery options do we have?
input.constraints: Owner has not been told. Internal-only analysis at this stage.

relevant_data.note: DH-2 Seq A topped out 2 days ahead — but mobilization is the gate to start.

expected_output.title: Schedule risk — DH-2 mobilization 2-week slip (QPB1)
expected_output.summary: DH-2 mobilization slipping 14 days burns total float (8 days) and pushes the critical path 6 days into the IT-handover buffer. Recommended: crash with a second steel-erection crew on Seq A — recovers 8 of 14 days for ~$1.4M, preserves the March 15 owner handover.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Situation

DH-2 mobilization is forecast 2026-06-01, 14 days behind the baseline of 2026-05-18. DH-2 currently shows 8 days of total float on its critical path (per the 2026-04-29 schedule pull). The owner-committed IT-equipment handover for DH-2 is 2027-03-15. DH-2 long-lead equipment is on or ahead of need-by: SWB-DH2-1A delivered 2026-04-30 (need-by 2026-09-10); chiller plant confirmed delivery 2026-10-22 (need-by 2026-11-10). The owner has not been informed; this is an internal-only analysis.

## Analysis

Drivers and math:

- A 14-day mobilization slip translates one-for-one to Seq A topout because mobilization is the single gating activity for steel erection on DH-2.
- Total float on the DH-2 critical path is 8 days. A 14-day slip burns the float and pushes the critical path 6 days past baseline.
- Downstream, the 6-day push lands on Seq B steel start (currently baselined 2026-09-15) before it can be absorbed by float in interior MEP rough-in. Interior MEP rough-in has historically run with ~10 days of internal float on QPB1 sister buildings (DH-1 trend).
- Net impact at IT-handover: ~6 days against a baseline of 30 days of project-level buffer ahead of 2027-03-15. The handover commitment is not at risk in the base case — but the buffer drops from 30 to ~24 days.
- Recovery levers: (a) crash Seq A with a second steel-erection crew; (b) compress interior MEP rough-in by overlapping trades; (c) re-baseline Seq B start. Levers (a) and (b) are real; lever (c) is the silent way to hide the slip and is not recommended.
- Procurement is not a constraint: switchgear is on site; chillers ship 19 days before need-by even with the slip.

Math on Option A (second steel crew on Seq A):

- Second crew premium: ~$95k/wk × 6 weeks of erection = ~$570k.
- Tower crane second shift: ~$120k/wk × 6 weeks = ~$720k.
- Travel-trade & per diem uplift: ~$110k.
- Total order-of-magnitude: ~$1.4M.
- Recovery: ~8 of 14 days based on DH-1 productivity benchmarks for double-crewed steel erection.

## Options

1. **Crash with a second steel-erection crew on Seq A.** Recover ~8 of 14 days; net residual slip ~6 days, absorbed by interior-MEP float. ~$1.4M order of magnitude. Owner handover preserved with ~24 days of buffer.
2. **Crash interior MEP rough-in by overlapping trades.** Recover ~5 of 14 days; net residual slip ~9 days, eats most of interior-MEP float. ~$0.6M. Higher coordination risk; subcontractor chemistry on QPB1 has been mixed.
3. **Accept the slip and re-baseline Seq B start.** No additional cost. Net 6 days into the handover buffer; owner not formally told. Hidden risk: if the next slip is even 3 days, handover slips. Strongly not recommended.
4. **No action / hold for more data.** Default if the May 4 schedule pull shows mobilization recovering. Leaves the 14-day slip in place; loses the option to crash if confirmed two weeks later.

## Recommendation

**Crash with a second steel-erection crew on Seq A.** The ~$1.4M premium is small versus the IT-handover commitment exposure and preserves credibility with the owner. The lever is real (second crew + crane second shift are sourceable on the New Albany trade base, per the April 21 trade-availability memo). Decision needed within 7 days: a second-crew mobilization needs lead time, and the lever erodes if we wait for the next schedule pull.

## Risks

- Productivity assumption (8 of 14 days recovery) is benchmark-based, not measured on DH-2. If recovery is closer to 5 days, residual slip eats interior-MEP float.
- Trade-base saturation in late summer 2026 could erode crew availability; mitigation by locking the second crew with a confirmed ramp-up date in the next two weeks.
- Hidden second slip risk: any further mobilization push on top of the 14 days exhausts levers.
- Owner-not-told risk: if Option 1 is approved internally, the next owner status update should disclose both the slip and the corrective action; concealing it is a credibility risk.

## Next Steps

1. Decision meeting on Option 1 vs. Option 2 by 2026-05-08 (project director + GC superintendent + procurement lead).
2. If Option 1 approved: lock second steel crew with subcontractor by 2026-05-12; tower-crane second shift confirmed by 2026-05-14.
3. Update the next weekly status update (2026-05-09) to disclose the slip and the corrective plan to the owner.
4. Re-pull schedule on 2026-05-04 to confirm mobilization forecast before the decision meeting.

input.question: What should our cash flow forecast look like through Q3 2026 (Jul–Sep)? Owner is asking us to validate the projected billings and the contingency drawdown ahead of the August funding tranche.
input.constraints: Memo will be packaged for the owner's funding committee. Page limit ~3 pages. Avoid speculative numbers; show the math.

relevant_data.note: Need-by 2026-11-10; delivery 2026-10-22; 50% milestone payment due Aug 2026 ~$22M
Delivered 2026-04-30; final 10% holdback ~$3M payable on commissioning Sep
No CO executed in period; CO-014 (chiller substitution) under review

expected_output.title: Cash flow forecast — Q3 2026 (QPB1)
expected_output.summary: Q3 2026 forecast: ~$1.18B billings (Jul $0.39B, Aug $0.42B, Sep $0.37B). $50M of contingency drawdown anticipated, leaving $95M at end of Q3 vs $145M start. Recommend approving the August funding tranche at full request; no re-baselining needed at this time.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Situation

Owner has asked QPB1 PMO to validate the Q3 2026 cash flow forecast (Jul–Sep) ahead of the August funding tranche. As of the 2026-04-30 cost report, $1.84B is committed against the $10.0B baseline; forecast at completion is $9.92B (-0.8%). Contingency remaining is $145M. Q2 ramp is driven by DH-1 underslab MEP rough-in and the May generator-yard slab pour. Q3 ramp is driven by DH-2 Seq A topout and milestone payments on long-lead equipment (genset, transformer, DH-2 chiller).

## Analysis

**Top-down:** GMP $10.0B over 30-month construction yields a smooth ~$333M/mo. Q3 falls in the steel-up phase — historically 1.15×–1.25× the smoothed rate on hyperscale jobs of this profile. That puts the smoothed Q3 forecast at $1.15B–$1.25B.

**Bottom-up build (Jul, Aug, Sep):**

- **Jul 2026 — $390M** (DH-1 finishes pull through, DH-2 mobilization & early steel; vendor milestone payments).
  - Construction progress billings ~$340M (DH-1 above-grade & MEP rough-in, DH-2 Seq A early erection).
  - Long-lead milestones ~$28M (genset G1 factory-test acceptance; payment terms 50% on test).
  - Travel-trade premium ~$3M (per Apr 21 trade memo).
  - Other (general conditions, OFCI rebill) ~$19M.

- **Aug 2026 — $420M** (DH-2 Seq A peak erection; DH-2 chiller milestone).
  - Construction progress billings ~$365M (DH-2 Seq A topout drives largest single-month billing of the period).
  - Long-lead milestones ~$22M (DH-2 chiller plant 50% milestone; per the procurement schedule).
  - Travel-trade premium ~$3M.
  - General conditions / OFCI ~$30M.

- **Sep 2026 — $370M** (DH-1 commissioning starts; DH-2 Seq B steel ramp).
  - Construction progress billings ~$330M.
  - Long-lead milestones ~$11M (transformer T-DH1-1 factory-test acceptance from June, billed Sep on net-90 terms; ~$3M switchgear SWB-DH1-1A 10% holdback released on DH-1 commissioning).
  - Travel-trade premium ~$2M.
  - General conditions / OFCI ~$24M.

**Q3 total — ~$1.18B billings**, inside the top-down band ($1.15B–$1.25B).

**Contingency drawdown.** $50M anticipated draw across Q3, driven by:

- ~$15M trade-premium absorption (already approved, posted against contingency line).
- ~$22M against currently open weather-loss recovery commitments accrued YTD (recognized as the work is performed).
- ~$10M against minor scope clarifications already in the RFI funnel; no new owner-impact CO anticipated.
- ~$3M reserve held for CO-014 (chiller substitution) if approved as drafted; if rejected, this reverts.

Contingency remaining at end of Q3 forecast: $95M ($145M start − $50M Q3 draw). This is 1.0% of remaining at-completion forecast — within the 0.8%–1.5% band typical for this stage of a hyperscale build.

## Options

1. **Approve the August funding tranche at full requested amount.** Q3 forecast supports it; FAC is favorable; contingency band is healthy. Recommended.
2. **Approve the August tranche and accelerate the Sep tranche by 30 days to cover any chiller substitution decision lag.** Pulls ~$11M earlier; minor working-capital cost. Worth considering only if CO-014 disposition slips past Aug 15.
3. **Approve the August tranche but flag a Q4 re-baselining trigger if contingency draw exceeds $60M in Q3.** Sets a clean owner-PMO check-in. Prudent overlay on Option 1.
4. **Hold the August tranche pending chiller-substitution disposition.** Not recommended — the chiller decision is not on the construction critical path through Aug, and a held tranche disrupts payable cycles.

## Recommendation

Recommend **Option 1 with the Option 3 overlay**: approve the August tranche at full requested amount, with an explicit trigger that QPB1 returns to the funding committee mid-Q3 if contingency draw exceeds $60M (vs forecast $50M) by August 15. The forecast is grounded: top-down and bottom-up agree, the long-lead milestone payments are tied to procurement records that exist, and the contingency band is within hyperscale norms for this stage. Holding the tranche (Option 4) creates payable-cycle risk for negligible upside.

## Risks

- Bottom-up forecast assumes DH-2 mobilization holds; the open 14-day-slip analysis (separate memo, 2026-05-01) could push ~$25M of Aug billings into Sep.
- CO-014 (chiller substitution) — currently $3M reserve held; if approved as a $14M scope add, contingency draw would reach $61M and trip the Option 3 trigger.
- Travel-trade premium is currently approved through Aug; an extension into Sep–Oct would add ~$4M not in this forecast.
- Long-lead milestone payments depend on factory-test acceptance dates; a slip in the genset G1 May 22 test would push ~$28M from Jul into Aug.

## Next Steps

1. Submit memo to owner funding committee for August tranche approval.
2. Set up the contingency-draw $60M trigger as a standing report in the weekly PMO cadence.
3. Re-issue this analysis on 2026-08-01 with actuals through July to confirm Q3 trend.
4. Cross-reference with the open DH-2 mobilization slip analysis; if Option 1 there is approved, refresh the Aug billing line.

input.question: What's the impact of the Trane CenTraVac substitution request submitted by the mechanical sub for the DH-2 chiller plant? They're proposing to substitute 4× CVHF-1500 units for the spec'd 4× York YK-1500 units, citing 12-week delivery improvement. Need scope, cost, schedule, performance, and risk analysis.
input.constraints: Engineer of Record (EOR) has not yet been formally consulted. Vendor has tendered a $0 net cost-neutral proposal but it does not include freight/integration delta.

relevant_data.note: Trane proposes 4× CVHF-1500 vs 4× York YK-1500
York basis-of-design; allows substitution per Section 01 25 13
Current vendor (York) confirmed delivery 2026-10-22; Trane offers 2026-08-01
York YK-1500 IPLV 0.32 kW/ton (basis); CVHF-1500 IPLV 0.34 kW/ton per manufacturer

expected_output.title: Impact analysis — DH-2 chiller substitution (Trane CVHF-1500 vs York YK-1500)
expected_output.summary: Substitution offers 12-week delivery pull-in but degrades IPLV efficiency 0.02 kW/ton (~$280k/yr energy cost). Net-cost-neutral claim is incomplete (freight + integration ~$1.4M not included). Recommend reject as proposed; counter for Trane to absorb integration delta and confirm efficiency parity test results.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Situation

Mechanical subcontractor submitted SUB-0488 on 2026-04-25 proposing substitution of the DH-2 chiller plant: 4× Trane CVHF-1500 in place of the 4× York YK-1500 that are basis-of-design (Spec 23 64 23, $48M PO QPB1-CHL-002 issued 2025-09). The proposal claims $0 net cost-neutral and a 12-week delivery improvement (2026-08-01 vs 2026-10-22 York delivery). EOR (RFI-00301) has not yet responded. Energy model was built on the York YK-1500 IPLV of 0.32 kW/ton; the CVHF-1500 manufacturer rating is 0.34 kW/ton.

## Analysis

**Schedule.** The 12-week pull-in is real on paper but the original York delivery (2026-10-22) is already 19 days ahead of need-by (2026-11-10). The schedule does not need the pull-in. Scheduled installation is gated by chiller-plant slab pour and overhead piping, not by chiller arrival. Net schedule benefit: zero on critical path; potential storage cost +$25k/mo for early arrival if accepted.

**Cost.** Vendor's $0 net-neutral claim excludes:

- Freight delta ~$240k (Wisconsin vs Pennsylvania OEM origin per published terms).
- Mechanical integration delta ~$1.1M (different connection size on chilled-water headers per CVHF spec sheet; 4 units × ~$275k field rework).
- Controls integration ~$80k (BAS BACnet object map differs).

Total hidden cost ~$1.4M. Add 50%-milestone refund logistics on the existing York PO (~$22M Aug 2026 milestone now held; potential restocking up to 8% if York refuses re-allocation = up to $1.76M further).

**Performance / Energy.** IPLV 0.32 → 0.34 kW/ton = 6.25% efficiency loss. Energy model run 2025-08-12 used 0.32. At DH-2 forecast load of 14,000 ton-hours/day, the delta is ~$280k/yr in energy cost over a 25-year horizon, NPV ~$3.6M at 8% discount rate.

**Risk.** Performance test results submitted by Trane are factory-bench, not AHRI 550/590 certified for this exact configuration on the proposed evaporator. Substitution acceptability is the EOR's call (Spec 01 25 13).

## Options

1. **Reject as proposed; counter with conditions.** Counter for Trane to absorb the ~$1.4M integration delta and to provide AHRI 550/590 certified test data demonstrating IPLV ≤ 0.33 kW/ton. If Trane accepts both, re-evaluate. Recommended.
2. **Accept the substitution as written.** Realize the 12-week pull-in (no critical-path benefit) and absorb ~$1.4M integration cost + ~$3.6M lifetime energy NPV cost ourselves. Strongly not recommended.
3. **Reject outright; hold York.** Preserves design basis. No upside, no downside beyond keeping the existing schedule.
4. **Defer pending EOR formal response (RFI-00301).** Reasonable holding pattern but loses 4–7 days of negotiation runway with Trane.

## Recommendation

**Option 1 — reject as proposed; counter with conditions.** The $0 net-neutral claim is materially incomplete: ~$1.4M of integration delta is not in the proposal, and the 6% efficiency degradation has a $3.6M lifetime NPV the owner will absorb. The 12-week schedule pull-in is not critical-path-relevant. A counter that asks Trane to absorb integration cost AND certify efficiency parity makes this a real option; without those concessions it's a giveaway.

## Risks

- Trane walks; lose the option entirely. Likelihood low — vendor is incentivized.
- York refuses to release the $22M milestone or imposes restocking. Likelihood medium; mitigation: cite Spec 23 64 23 substitution clause and PO Article 17.
- EOR rejects substitution on factory-test acceptability grounds. Likelihood medium; the analysis stands either way.
- Owner sees the substitution as an attempt to recover sub margin and pushes back. Likelihood low; transparency in our recommendation should preempt it.

## Next Steps

1. Issue counter-proposal letter to Trane with the two conditions above (integration absorption + AHRI cert).
2. Push EOR for RFI-00301 formal response within 7 days.
3. Pull the York PO's substitution and milestone-hold language; share with procurement counsel for the milestone-refund mechanics.
4. Brief project director and decline a same-week answer to the sub; this is a 2-week negotiation, not a same-week call.

input.question: Owner is asking us to add a 5th building (DH-5) to QPB1 scope, parallel-tracked with DH-4. They want it operational by Q4 2027. Lay out the analysis: scope/cost/schedule/risk implications, what we'd need from owner to even begin estimating, and our recommended path.
input.constraints: This is a verbal request from the owner during the May 1 site walk. No formal directive issued. Memo will go to the owner's program lead and Charles, not yet to the funding committee.

relevant_data.note: Site has provisional pad for DH-5 referenced as 'future expansion'; civil rough-grade complete
Defines current scope as 4 buildings, 1.7 GW total
Critical path runs through DH-4 chiller commissioning Aug 2027
All ordered for DH-1 through DH-4; no DH-5 equipment ordered
Owner program lead asked PM verbally; Charles present; no written directive

expected_output.title: Scope add analysis — DH-5 (5th building) Q4 2027 operational ask (QPB1)
expected_output.summary: DH-5 op by Q4 2027 is not feasible under current GMP: requires written directive, GMP amendment, ~$2.4–$2.8B add, and locks long-lead equipment (genset/switchgear/chiller) by Sep 2026 to hit a ~Q1 2028 best-case op date. Recommend: do not commit a date; respond with a structured estimate plan, not a yes.
expected_output.body_markdown:
## Situation

During the 2026-05-01 site walk, the owner program lead verbally asked QPB1 to add a 5th building (DH-5) to scope, parallel-tracked with DH-4, operational by Q4 2027. No written directive has been issued. The current GMP (2025-11-04) covers 4 buildings (DH-1 through DH-4) and 1.7 GW. Civil rough-grade for a DH-5 pad is complete and the pad is referenced on the C-100 series as 'future expansion.' All long-lead equipment to date has been ordered for DH-1 through DH-4 only; nothing for DH-5. Critical path on the current GMP runs through DH-4 chiller commissioning in August 2027.

This memo is a structured response to the verbal ask. It does not commit QPB1 to a Q4 2027 op date. It does not estimate a cost. It lays out what would be needed to even begin estimating, the schedule physics of the request, and the recommended path for QPB1 to take with the owner.

## Analysis

**Scope physics.** DH-5 at the same nameplate as DH-1–DH-4 (~425 MW) implies an equivalent program: ~3.5M sf white space, identical chiller plant + switchgear + genset complement, identical IT-handover commissioning. Civil pad is a head start (~$45M of site work already in place at the 2025 GMP rate); everything else is greenfield within the existing site.

**Order-of-magnitude cost.** At the current per-building burn (GMP $10.0B / 4 buildings ≈ $2.5B per building), DH-5 would land $2.4B–$2.8B with the civil credit and shared site infrastructure already in place. This is an order-of-magnitude estimate, not a quotable number; firm pricing requires a re-priced GMP package.

**Schedule physics.** From verbal ask (2026-05-01) to operational, a comparable hyperscale build of this size has historically taken ~24 months minimum from steel start. Steel start would require:

- Long-lead equipment ordered: chiller plant 14-month lead, switchgear 16-month, genset 18-month, transformer 20-month. Working back from a steel-start of Q1 2027, the long-lead PO window closes in Sep 2026.
- Design package complete: DH-5 currently has civil only. Architectural and MEP design is approximately 14 months from a kickoff. Working back from Q1 2027 IFC, design kickoff is now (May 2026).

Best-case operational date assuming all of the above tracks cleanly: ~Q1–Q2 2028. Q4 2027 is not feasible without (a) parallel design, (b) accepting design risk on long-lead equipment ordered to a 60% set, (c) site logistics constraints that conflict with DH-3 / DH-4 critical path activity in 2027.

**Risk to the existing program.** Pulling design, procurement, and management resources to DH-5 in May 2026 — when DH-2 mobilization is itself contested (open analysis 2026-05-01) — risks the existing Q3 2026 build. Owner attention being split between 4 buildings and a 5th risks the IT-handover commitments on DH-1, DH-2, DH-3.

**Contractual posture.** A verbal request is not a directive. QPB1 should respond with a structured what-we-need-to-estimate plan. Issuing any number off a verbal ask invites both anchor-pricing risk and contractual ambiguity.

## Options

1. **Acknowledge verbally; respond formally with a structured estimate plan within 14 days.** Asks owner for a written directive to fund the estimate work, names the predecessor decisions (Q4 2027 vs Q1 2028 op date trade), and outlines design + long-lead window. Recommended.
2. **Decline outright.** Answer the verbal ask with 'we can't do Q4 2027.' Closes the door cleanly but forfeits the relationship value of a structured response.
3. **Provide a verbal range estimate now.** Strongly not recommended — anchors the owner's expectations on a number with no basis.
4. **Begin design and procurement work on DH-5 in parallel.** Strongly not recommended without written directive — exposes QPB1 to spend with no contract cover.

## Recommendation

**Option 1.** Within 14 days, deliver a formal response memo with three components: (a) what we need from the owner to estimate (written directive funding the estimate, target op date trade-off Q4 2027 vs Q1–Q2 2028, IT-handover scope confirmation); (b) order-of-magnitude band ($2.4–$2.8B) framed as 'pre-estimate, requires GMP amendment to firm'; (c) schedule physics showing Q4 2027 requires accepting design risk on long-lead orders to a 60% set. Charles approves the memo before it goes to the owner program lead.

## Risks

- Verbal ask becomes 'we asked, you went silent' if QPB1 does not respond formally within ~14 days. Mitigation: Option 1 timeline.
- Owner anchors on the order-of-magnitude band as a quote. Mitigation: explicit language 'pre-estimate, not a quote; requires GMP amendment to firm.'
- Resourcing pull to DH-5 design in May 2026 erodes DH-2 mobilization recovery. Mitigation: scope the estimate work as engineering-only (design firm, no procurement actions) until written directive received.
- Owner perceives the structured response as obstruction. Mitigation: open the memo with what we CAN do (estimate plan) before what we need.

## Next Steps

1. Charles + Project Director align on response posture before the memo is drafted.
2. Comms Drafter (separate agent) drafts the formal response memo to the owner program lead, referencing this analysis.
3. Engineer of Record contacted for a design-package estimate (cost + duration to IFC for DH-5).
4. Procurement Lead pulls long-lead window dates so the owner sees the Sep 2026 PO trigger date concretely.
5. Open a placeholder 'DH-5 estimate work' line in the controls system; do not charge to it without written directive.

rfi.subject: CRAH unit drain pan slope — field condition
rfi.body: Cannot achieve 1/8" per foot slope due to BM-204. Advise.

spec_excerpts.text: Condensate drains shall slope at minimum 1/8 inch per foot to a primary drain or pumped condensate system.

contract_clauses.text: Field deviations from contract documents require an ASI.

expected_output.draft_response: Spec 23 31 13 §2.7.A allows either gravity slope (1/8 in/ft minimum) or a pumped condensate system. Three options are available given the BM-204 conflict shown on S2.04: (1) re-route the gravity drain along an alternate path that maintains slope (preferred if a feasible route exists); (2) install a packaged condensate pump at CRAH-DH2-04 routed to the nearest sanitary or condensate main; (3) modify BM-204 — not recommended without structural review. Submit the proposed solution as an ASI per Contract 01 25 00 §3.2 with cost and schedule impact for design team approval [Spec 23 31 13 §2.7.A; Dwg M3.04; Dwg S2.04; Contract 01 25 00 §3.2].
follow_up_questions: Confirm preferred option (re-route, pumped condensate, or beam modification) with cost and schedule impact for each.

cost_impact.basis: Order-of-magnitude based on packaged condensate pump (~$4-8k installed) vs re-routing labor (~$8-18k); to be refined upon contractor proposal.

- 2500 kVA transformer T-DH1-1 on track, factory test Jun 15, need-by Jun 30.

## Risks

- R-014 — DH-2 chiller delivery slip. Level P3. Owner: procurement lead. Weekly vendor call running; bridging plan with alternate cooling tower held.
- R-021 — Local trade availability for fall surge. Level P3. Owner: GC superintendent. Travel-trade premium approved Apr 21; 60 trades onboarded Apr 29.

spec_section.text:
Switchgear shall be manufactured by Eaton, ABB, or Siemens. No substitutions.

expected_output.title: Subcontract Agreement — Precision Framing Inc. — Riverview Commons Building C
expected_output.summary: This subcontract agreement dated June 1, 2026 governs rough and finish framing work on Building C of the Riverview Commons multifamily project. Summit Construction LLC (Contractor) engages Precision Framing Inc. (Subcontractor) to furnish all labor, materials, and supervision for framing per drawings S1.0–S4.2, for a fixed sum of $185,000. Payment flows on a pay-when-paid basis within 10 days of Contractor's receipt from Owner, with 5% retainage withheld through substantial completion. The subcontract incorporates all applicable Prime Contract terms.
expected_output.body_markdown:
# SUBCONTRACT AGREEMENT

**Project:** Riverview Commons — Building C
**Date:** June 1, 2026

---

## Article 1 — Parties and Project

This Subcontract Agreement ("Subcontract") is entered into as of **June 1, 2026** by and between:

**Contractor:** Summit Construction LLC
1234 Industrial Pkwy, Columbus, OH 43215

**Subcontractor:** Precision Framing Inc.
567 Maple Ave, Westerville, OH 43081

For work on the project known as **Riverview Commons — Building C** (the "Project"), located at [Project Address, Columbus, OH].

---

## Article 2 — Scope of Work

Subcontractor shall furnish all labor, materials, equipment, supervision, and services necessary to complete the following Scope of Work:

Rough and finish framing for a 24-unit multifamily residential building (Building C) at the Riverview Commons project, including installation of all dimensional lumber, engineered lumber, sheathing, and related blocking, in strict accordance with architectural drawings sheets S1.0–S4.2 and framing specifications section 06100.

This Subcontract covers Building C only; Buildings A and B are governed by separate subcontracts.

---

## Article 3 — Contract Documents; Flow-Down

This Subcontract is subject to the Prime Contract between Summit Construction LLC and the Owner. All terms of the Prime Contract applicable to the Subcontractor's Work are incorporated herein by reference. Subcontractor acknowledges receipt of all Prime Contract documents relevant to its scope and assumes all obligations toward the Contractor that the Contractor assumes toward the Owner with respect to the Work.

In the event of conflict: this Subcontract governs, then the Prime Contract.

---

## Article 4 — Subcontract Sum and Payment

The Contractor shall pay the Subcontractor the **Subcontract Sum of $185,000.00 (One Hundred Eighty-Five Thousand Dollars)** for the Subcontractor's full performance, subject to additions and deductions per this Subcontract.

**Progress Payments:** Contractor shall pay Subcontractor within **10 days** after Contractor receives payment from Owner for Subcontractor's Work (pay-when-paid). Subcontractor shall submit Applications for Payment by the 25th of each month.

**Retainage:** **5%** of each progress payment shall be withheld as retainage until Substantial Completion of Subcontractor's Work.

**Final Payment:** Final payment, constituting the remaining Subcontract Sum and accrued retainage, shall be made when: (a) Subcontractor's Work is fully complete; (b) all punch-list items are resolved; (c) Subcontractor has delivered all closeout documents including final lien waivers.

---

## Article 5 — Schedule and Time

Subcontractor shall perform its Work to support Contractor's overall project schedule. The Project's anticipated Substantial Completion date is **November 15, 2026**. Subcontractor shall commence framing operations promptly upon receipt of Contractor's notice to proceed and shall maintain adequate manpower to meet the approved schedule.

Subcontractor must notify Contractor in writing within 48 hours of any delay or condition that may impact the schedule.

---

## Article 6 — Changes

Contractor may order changes to the Subcontract Work by written Change Order. No extra work shall be performed without a written Change Order signed by Contractor. Changes shall be priced by mutual agreement (lump sum, unit price, or time and materials with a cap). Subcontractor may seek equitable adjustment in Subcontract Sum and Time for Owner-caused or Force Majeure delays, subject to the Prime Contract's change order procedures.

---

## Article 7 — Safety

Subcontractor is solely responsible for the safety of its own employees and subcontractors. Subcontractor shall comply with all applicable OSHA standards and Contractor's site safety program. Subcontractor shall attend site safety meetings as required by Contractor.

---

## Article 8 — Insurance

Subcontractor shall maintain the following minimum insurance coverages throughout the Subcontract:

- **Commercial General Liability:** $2,000,000 per occurrence / $4,000,000 aggregate
- **Automobile Liability:** $500,000 combined single limit
- **Workers' Compensation:** Statutory limits; **Employer's Liability:** $1,000,000

**Summit Construction LLC** and the **Owner** shall be named as additional insureds on Subcontractor's CGL policy on a primary, non-contributory basis. Subcontractor shall provide certificates of insurance before commencing Work. Subcontractor's insurer shall provide 30 days' notice of cancellation.

---

## Article 9 — Indemnification

Subcontractor shall defend, indemnify, and hold harmless Contractor and Owner from and against any claims, damages, losses, and expenses arising out of or resulting from the **Subcontractor's own negligent acts, omissions, or willful misconduct** in connection with the Work. This indemnification does not extend to losses caused by the negligence of Contractor or Owner.

*Note: This indemnification clause is intentionally limited consistent with Ohio Revised Code §4113.62, which restricts the enforceability of construction indemnification clauses requiring one party to indemnify for another's negligence.*

---

## Article 10 — Warranties

Subcontractor warrants its Work against defects in materials and workmanship for a period of **one (1) year** from the date of Substantial Completion of the Project. Subcontractor shall correct any defective Work at its own expense during the warranty period.

---

## Article 11 — Dispute Resolution

Any dispute arising out of or relating to this Subcontract shall be resolved by **binding arbitration** administered by the American Arbitration Association under its Construction Industry Arbitration Rules. The arbitration shall be seated in **Franklin County, Ohio**. The arbitrator's award shall be final and may be entered as judgment in any court of competent jurisdiction.

This Subcontract shall be governed by the laws of the **State of Ohio**.

---

## Article 12 — Termination

**For Cause:** Contractor may terminate this Subcontract for cause upon written notice if Subcontractor: (a) persistently fails to perform the Work in accordance with the Contract Documents; (b) abandons the Work; (c) becomes insolvent or files for bankruptcy. Subcontractor shall have 48 hours to begin curing a default before termination is effective (except for abandonment or insolvency).

**For Convenience:** Contractor may terminate this Subcontract for convenience upon 5 days' written notice. Upon termination for convenience, Subcontractor shall be entitled to payment for all Work properly performed to the date of termination, plus reasonable demobilization costs. Subcontractor shall not be entitled to anticipated profits on unperformed work.

---

## Article 13 — Lien Waivers

Subcontractor shall deliver a partial conditional lien waiver with each Application for Payment, covering all Work through the date of that application. Subcontractor shall deliver an unconditional final lien waiver upon receipt of final payment. Subcontractor shall require the same from all of its sub-subcontractors and material suppliers.

---

## Article 14 — Miscellaneous

- **Entire Agreement:** This Subcontract, together with the Prime Contract documents incorporated by reference, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties as to the subject matter hereof.
- **Amendments:** No amendment is binding unless in writing and signed by both parties.
- **Waiver:** No waiver is effective unless in writing.
- **Assignment:** Subcontractor may not assign this Subcontract without Contractor's prior written consent.
- **Severability:** If any provision is unenforceable, the remaining provisions remain in full force.
- **Notices:** Notices must be in writing, delivered by email with confirmation, overnight courier, or hand delivery.

---

By: _________________________ Date: __________
Printed Name: _________________________
Title: _________________________

By: _________________________ Date: __________

Title: _________________________

## 1. Statements of Work

All services performed under this Agreement shall be described in one or more written Statements of Work ("SOW") executed by both parties. Each SOW shall specify at minimum: scope of services, deliverables, timeline, fees, and acceptance criteria. In the event of conflict between an SOW and this Agreement, the SOW governs for that engagement.

## 2. Fees and Payment

Service Provider shall invoice Client per the fee schedule in each SOW. Client shall pay undisputed invoices within **30 days** of receipt. Late payments bear interest at 1.5% per month (18% per annum) on amounts outstanding after the due date.

If Client disputes any invoice amount, Client shall notify Service Provider in writing within 10 days of receipt, specifying the disputed amount and basis. The parties shall work in good faith to resolve disputes within 15 days. Undisputed amounts shall be paid timely regardless of any dispute.

## 3. Intellectual Property

**3.1 Client Ownership of Deliverables.** Subject to Section 3.2 and full payment of fees, all deliverables created specifically for Client under each SOW ("Client Deliverables") are works made for hire to the extent permitted by applicable law. To the extent any Client Deliverable does not qualify as a work made for hire, Service Provider hereby assigns all right, title, and interest, including all intellectual property rights, in such Deliverable to Client upon final payment.

**3.2 Service Provider Pre-Existing IP.** Service Provider retains ownership of all tools, frameworks, templates, methods, code libraries, and generic creative assets developed independently of this Agreement ("Pre-Existing IP").
